hypotheses: Social observation will induce a deontological decision bias in moral dilemmas, by making participants more tuned to signaling aspects of moral judgments related to warmth.
The deontological bias induced by social observation will be present for moral (but not non-moral) dilemmas and will be more pronounced for personal moral dilemmas than impersonal ones.
Social observation will increase reaction times (RTs) due to cognitive burden from reputation concern and self-monitoring.

Social observation will increase sensitivity (faster RTs) in a lexical decision task to reputation-related words, including warmth- and competence-related traits (with predicted enhanced reactivity toward warmth and competence words).
claim.statement: Social observation increases deontological judgments in moral dilemmas
claim.statement_location: Title and Abstract (paper title: 'Social observation increases deontological judgments in moral dilemmas'; Abstract states the main claim).

data.source: Behavioral experiment with human participants performing moral dilemma tasks (pretest PD dilemmas and main moral dilemma task), a lexical decision task (LDT), and self-report questionnaires (IRI, Need for Cognition, FNE, mood/arousal).
data.sample_size: 48 participants (24 in observed group, 24 in control group).
data.notes: Stimuli translated into Korean; pretest used 20 PD dilemmas to estimate baseline D and U via process dissociation (PD) analysis; main task used 50 dilemmas (20 personal moral, 15 impersonal moral, 15 non-moral control); tasks implemented using Cogent 2000 toolbox in MATLAB (version 8.4.0). No public dataset or code repository link provided in the paper; supplementary materials referenced online.

method.description: A mixed-design laboratory experiment. Participants completed a pretest of 20 PD moral dilemmas to estimate baseline deontological (D) and utilitarian (U) inclinations. Participants were then randomly assigned to an Observed (OBS) group or a Control (CON) group (N=24 per group). The main task presented 50 moral and non-moral dilemmas (personal, impersonal, non-moral); OBS participants were led to believe two experimenters would observe and record their performance, while CON participants were told responses would remain anonymous. After the main task participants performed a lexical decision task (LDT) with word categories (Reputation, Control, Warmth, Competence, Non-word). Post-experiment questionnaires (IRI, Need for Cognition, Fear of Negative Evaluation, mood/arousal) were collected. Behavioral measures analyzed included proportion of deontological responses, decision confidence ratings, reaction times in dilemma decisions, and LDT reaction times.
steps: Recruit participants from volunteer pool; screen for prior exposure to moral dilemma literature and advanced psychology coursework; obtain informed consent.

Pretest: present 20 PD moral dilemmas in randomized order; collect binary moral judgments (appropriate/inappropriate) and 4-point confidence ratings; compute baseline D and U parameters via process dissociation analysis.

Randomly assign participants to OBS or CON groups (24 each); provide group-specific instructions including social observation manipulation (OBS: told performances will be monitored/recorded by two observers with monitoring device; CON: told responses will be encrypted and anonymous).

Main task: present 50 dilemmas (20 personal, 15 impersonal, 15 non-moral) in randomized order; collect binary moral judgments, confidence ratings, and reaction times (latency from decision prompt to response).
Lexical Decision Task (LDT): Go/no-go paradigm; present words from Reputation, Control, Warmth, Competence, and Non-word categories; collect RTs for correct trials and hit rates.
Post-experiment questionnaires: IRI, Need for Cognition, FNE, and post-task mood/arousal ratings.

Statistical analysis: compute PD parameters; run mixed ANOVAs (group × dilemma type, etc.), paired t-tests with Bonferroni correction for post-hoc tests, and Pearson correlations for exploratory analyses.
method.models: Process dissociation (PD) analysis to estimate deontological (D) and utilitarian (U) inclinations; mixed-design ANOVAs (e.g., 2 × 3 mixed ANOVA on proportion deontological responses; 2 × 3 × 2 mixed ANOVAs for confidence and RTs; 2 × 4 mixed ANOVA for LDT RTs); Bonferroni-corrected paired t-tests for post-hoc comparisons; independent-samples t-tests; Pearson correlations. Effect sizes reported as Cohen's d and partial eta-squared (η2p).
method.outcome_variable: Primary outcomes: proportion of deontological responses in the main moral dilemma task; secondary outcomes: decision confidence ratings, reaction times for dilemma decisions, reaction times in lexical decision task (by word category), and questionnaire scores.
method.independent_variables: Between-subjects: Group (Observed vs Control). Within-subjects: Dilemma type (Personal, Impersonal, Non-moral), Response type (Appropriate vs Inappropriate) for some analyses, LDT word category (Reputation, Control, Warmth, Competence).
method.control_variables: Baseline moral orientation (D-parameter) included in some models as covariate; sex and motor-counterbalancing tested and reported but not included in final models as they did not produce significant effects; mood and arousal measured pre/post; individual difference measures (IRI, Need for Cognition, FNE) collected for exploratory correlation analyses.
method.tools_software: Cogent 2000 toolbox in MATLAB (version 8.4.0) for task implementation; SPSS 23.0 for statistical analyses; G*Power 3.1 for a priori power analysis.

results.summary: Social observation increased deontological judgments specifically for personal moral dilemmas. No baseline differences in D or U parameters between groups were found. Observed-group participants showed lower overall decision confidence and longer reaction times across dilemmas. Lexical decision task results showed that observed-group participants responded faster to warmth-related trait words relative to competence words (reduced Warmth/Competence RT ratio), indicating increased sensitivity to warmth-related constructs; reputation-category results were not directly associated with moral judgments. Exploratory correlations indicated that fear of negative evaluation (FNE) correlated with the Warmth/Competence RT ratio only in the Observed group.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Proportion of deontological responses (personal moral dilemmas)
numerical_results.effect_size: Cohen's d = 0.70 for OBS vs CON contrast
numerical_results.p_value: p = .018 (two-sample t-test comparing OBS vs CON for personal dilemmas)
numerical_results.direction: positive (OBS > CON; increased deontological responses under observation)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Interaction: Group × Dilemma type on proportion deontological responses
numerical_results.direction: interaction showing selective increase in deontological responses for personal dilemmas in OBS group
negative (OBS < CON; lower confidence under observation)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Reaction time (group main effect, across dilemmas)
numerical_results.value: OBS mean RT = 3240.93 ms; CON mean RT = 2592.78 ms

numerical_results.outcome_name: Lexical Decision Task: Warmth category RT (OBS vs CON)
numerical_results.value: OBS mean = 538.38 ms; CON mean = 592.26 ms
numerical_results.p_value: p = .040 (OBS faster than CON for Warmth words)
numerical_results.direction: negative (OBS faster RTs for Warmth words)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Lexical Decision Task: Warmth/Competence RT ratio (W/C)
numerical_results.p_value: p = .006 (difference in W/C ratio between groups)
numerical_results.direction: OBS < CON (relative facilitation for Warmth vs Competence in OBS)

numerical_results.outcome_name: Pretest baseline D and U parameters (group comparison)
numerical_results.value: No significant group differences (D and U standardized parameters equivalent across OBS and CON)

metadata.original_paper_title: Social observation increases deontological judgments in moral dilemmas
